prevalence.source: Orphanet rare disease summaries; Brazilian inborn errors of metabolism reporting (specialty centers)
prevalence.trend: stable; detection improving with expanded metabolic testing/newborn screening pilots

incidence.source: International IEM incidence syntheses; tertiary metabolic clinic case series

burden.dalyPerCapita: very low (rare), but high per-patient morbidity (neurodevelopmental injury, renal disease)
burden.mortalityRate: Low overall; higher in untreated severe neonatal-onset forms
burden.source: IHME/GBD rare disease context (modeled); Orphanet clinical burden summaries

riskFactors: family history of inborn errors of metabolism
vulnerablePopulations: patients with limited access to specialized metabolic care
epidemiology.diagnosticCriteria: Elevated methylmalonic acid (plasma/urine), metabolic acidosis and hyperammonemia during crises, acylcarnitine profile (C3), confirmatory enzymatic/genetic testing; assess B12 responsiveness

publicHealthSystem.screeningCoverage: variable; expanded newborn screening and confirmatory testing concentrated in reference centers
publicHealthSystem.estimatedPatients: Hundreds to low thousands (rare disease; uncertain)
publicHealthSystem.controlRate: Moderate when diagnosed early and followed by metabolic teams; poor if diagnosis delayed

regionalSpecificities.diagnosisCriteria: Referral to metabolic genetics centers; confirmatory MS/MS and genetic testing may require out-of-state travel
regionalSpecificities.treatmentProtocol: Emergency sick-day plans, protein-restricted diet with medical formulas, carnitine; B12 (hydroxocobalamin) if responsive; management of renal/neurologic complications
regionalSpecificities.regionalChallenges: Delayed diagnosis, uneven availability of metabolic formulas and specialized follow-up, limited adult-transition care
regionalSpecificities.strongPoints: Growing rare disease networks and reference centers; improved access to tandem mass spectrometry in major cities
